retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periosteum And Endosteum -- Tissue Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Ground Section of the Bone : Traditionally, the ground section is used to observe the histology of the bone without staining. In the ground section, the bone can be examined histologically without calcification. Steps for the ground section of the bon e: The specimen is placed 20% formalin solution for 24 hours, washed with tap water. The bone is sectioned to the desired thickness by any of the following methods. 1. Ultramicrotome with a diamond cutting blade.  2. Grinding with burr from both sides. 3. Hand grinding using a carborundum stone. A continuous spray of water and Paris powder is recommended while grinding the tissue to avoid damage to the histology of the tissue. Advantage of the Ground section method: Minimal minerals are lost during the method. Disadvantage : Ultrathin sections are difficult to achieve. [44] Other Methods for Undecalcified Bone Preparation for Periosteum and Endosteum Study [45] [46] [47] [48] :

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cementicle is a condition in which masses of acellular cementum appear in abnormal locations due to the disruption of the oppositional growth of cementum. This condition happens mostly in the elderly and appears as psammoma body-like calcifications. The process is thought to develop due to the encounter of cementoblasts with thrombus lodged in a nearby capillary. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periosteum And Endosteum -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Bone growth: During the development of the bone, the periosteum is thick and contains osteoprogenitor cells; Osteoblasts differentiated from the osteoprogenitor cells are crucial for the appositional growth of the bone. [18] Endosteum plays a role in the formation of an internal matrix by absorption and deposition of tissue. [6] The endosteum stimulates the uninterrupted internal bone resorption. The medullary canal, along with the overall bone diameter, increases because of endosteum-stimulated resorption. [31] [32] Endosteal endoblasts secrete bone matrix and compose ridges beside the periosteal blood vessels. The bony ridges expand and fuse to convert the groove into a vascularized tunnel. Endosteal osteoblasts compose new lamellae and form new osteons. Finally, a new circumferential lamella appears beneath the periosteum. This process repeats for continuous bone diameter enlargement, which slows down with adulthood. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periosteum And Endosteum -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Bone modeling and remodeling: Periosteum, endosteum, and its cells play a critical role in modeling and remodeling. Bone remodeling is a process where osteoclasts and osteoblasts work sequentially to reshape and renew bone; the process continues throughout life. It divides into four phases; (a) recruitment and activation of osteoclasts, (b) resorption of old bony tissue, (c) apoptosis of osteoclasts, and activation of osteoblasts (d) laying of new organic matrix and mineralization. [37] [38] [39] Bone modeling is a process to shape the bone during growth, development, and healing. Mechanical factors like stress, strain, tension, muscular attachment, etc. play an important role in bone modeling. Osteoblasts and osteoclast play an independent role in bone modeling. [37] [11] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Alveolar Osteitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The immune system's response to tooth extraction is comparable to any other tissue trauma. The inflammatory response in the alveolus involves pro-and anti-inflammatory cytokines. Studies have primarily focused on the effects of IL-6. IL-6 has a dual role in bone formation and bone resorption, which could contribute to the fibrinolysis in alveolar osteitis. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Odontology -- Clinical Significance -- Age Determination Based on Dental Data
retrieved_docs.content: In the year, 1947 a technique was developed based on structural tooth changes by Gustafson. [8] According to his technique, there are six criteria. Those include attrition, amount of recession in gingiva, secondary dentin formation, apposition of cementum, resorption, and transparency of root. These alterations were itemized with a predetermined score ranging from zero (0) to three (3), and an equation put together for linear regression aimed at calculating the age of an individual. The relationship constant achieved was 0.98. This technique is considered as the standard for human age estimation and is being used ever since its development. The core shortcoming of Gustafson method was the setting of equality for all the standards for equivalency.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mesial drift : Also known as physiological drift, this refers to the migration of teeth in a mesial direction caused by the asymmetrical remodeling of alveolar bone tissue. Two major concepts are believed to cause this drift: First, unbalanced bone remodeling of 2 adjacent teeth. Second, remodeling due to occlusal forces leads to an imbalance of acellular cementum. This asymmetrical remodeling causes tooth movement. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Cementum is similar to bone in structure and composition, has cementocytes that occupy lacunae, and is composed of an inorganic substance and mineralized organic material. With aging, many cementocytes die, and the cementum loses its regenerative population of cells. A frequent marker of aging is cementicles, small round calcified bodies on the cementum or periodontal ligament. The physical properties of the dentin-enamel junction (DEJ) have been examined, and the tensile cohesive strength of this structure is 51.5 MPa. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periosteum And Endosteum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone is a specialized complex, living connective tissue that supports the body and protects vital organs of the body. [1] [2] Impregnation of the extracellular matrix with the inorganic salts like calcium phosphate and carbonate provides hardness to the bone. [3] Types of Bones: Histologically, bones categorize into two types (1) cortical or compact bone and (2) cancellous bone or spongy bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The periodontium is also significant because of its effect on systemic health and environmental risk factors affecting it. Some periodontium medications include anti-convulsants (diphenylhydantoin), immunosuppressants, corticosteroids, NSAIDs, and CCBs. [17] These drugs could directly affect the periodontium by increasing matrix production or decreasing its degradation and, as a result, cause gingival hyperplasia. [18] Smoking and bacterial biofilms within periodontal pockets are common causes of attachment loss between cementum and alveolar bone, promoting tooth loss. [19] Periodontal diseases have also been studied as a risk for dental implants over time. [20] This study has shown that periodontal status and smoking are important contributors to long-term implant failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Hungry Bone Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ge et al retrospectively assessed bone turnover markers in patients with HBS following parathyroidectomy for secondary hyperparathyroidism. The study found that, before parathyroidectomy, patients with HBS had significantly lower levels of osteocalcin—a marker of bone formation—and significantly higher levels of tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase-5b (TRAP-5b)—a bone resorption marker—compared to those without HBS. [9] Additionally, post-parathyroidectomy, all patients showed a statistically significant shift in bone markers, with an increase in markers of bone formation (osteocalcin, calcitonin, and C-terminal peptide) and a decrease in markers of bone resorption (TRAP-5b). During this process, the shift in bone metabolism from resorption to net formation leads to an influx of minerals into the bone, resulting in depleted levels of calcium and phosphate in the blood. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bone -- Function -- Bone Cells
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoprogenitor (stem) cells: Osteoprogenitor cells retain the ability to re-differentiate into osteoblasts. They reside in the bone canals, endosteum, periosteum, and marrow. They may regulate the influx and efflux of mineral ions into and out of the bone extracellular matrix. They also are responsible for the formation of bone remodeling compartments (BRC) with a specialized microenvironment. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Osseous Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: A variant of osseous surgery is fiber retention osseous resective surgery, where instead of a full-thickness flap, a split-thickness flap is used to preserve attachment fibers on root surfaces before conservative bone removal. [35] This technique results in similar pocket depth reduction but less gingival recession and dentinal hypersensitivity. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periosteum And Endosteum -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Endosteum : A membrane lining the inner surface of the bony wall also identified as the lining membrane of the Bone marrow cavity is endosteum; The endosteum lines the Haversian canal and all the internal cavities of the bone. The endosteum consists of a layer of flattened osteoprogenitor cells and a type-III collagenous fibers (reticular fibers). [23] [18] The endosteum is noticeably thinner than the periosteum. [24] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Concrescence: It is the fusion of two teeth at the cementum region only. It may occur either during root formation termed as true/developmental concrescence or after the completion of root formation termed as acquired/inflammatory concrescence. Unlike fusion, concrescence commonly occurs in the posterior region of the maxilla. The union of cementum may vary in degree from one small site to the involvement of the entire root surface. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Bone Defect Morphology
retrieved_docs.content: Shallow vertical bone defects range from uneven crestal bone to defects of approximately 3 mm deep, easily recognized on radiographs. [11] A defect is considered deep if it is greater than 3 mm. Three-wall defects are typically found in posterior areas with wide interproximal bone consisting of a buccal, lingual, and interproximal wall. Three-wall defects may also be found at buccal or lingual furcations facing a bone shelf or exostosis. As the mesiodistal volume of the interproximal bone decreases, 3-wall defects may merge and form 2-wall defects called interproximal craters, where only the buccal and lingual walls remain. A 2-wall defect with a buccal and interproximal wall may develop adjacent to the entrance of the interproximal furcation of maxillary molars.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 462
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are closely linked to inflammation (الالْتِهاب) and to bone resorption (resorpce kosti). These associations indicate that inflammatory processes and the loss of bone tissue are central mechanisms underlying a range of skeletal disorders and deformities. Recognizing the connection between inflammation and bone resorption can guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving bone integrity and preventing further musculoskeletal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: activity, phosphate chelation, inhibition of bone resorption) and their synergistic inclusion with iron, calcium, other vitamins and trace minerals underpin their therapeutic, preventive and formulation‑stabilizing roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 507
retrieved_docs.content: network of relationships illustrates how hormonal deficiencies, drug classifications, biomarker concepts, and immunomodulatory agents intersect in clinical pharmacology, patient management, and the design of biomaterial‑based interventions for bone regeneration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: الالْتِهاب (inflammation) is associated with other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities, indicating that inflammatory processes can contribute to the development or worsening of skeletal disorders and structural abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as addressing underlying inflammation may help prevent or mitigate bone pathology and deformities, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin is biologically related to parathormone, the principal parathyroid hormone. Parathormone is classified within the broader group of parathyroid hormones and analogues, a therapeutic class that also encompasses the synthetic analogue teriparatide. Understanding the connections among calcitonin, parathormone, and their related analogues aids clinicians in comprehending calcium‑regulating pathways and in selecting appropriate hormone‑based treatments for disorders of bone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical planning, and coordinated management of fractures and bone‑density disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on bone is the classification category that includes the surgical intervention known as total ostectomy of tarsals and metatarsals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon is classified as a member of the parathyroid hormones and analogues group, indicating that it shares the biological characteristics and therapeutic relevance typical of this class of hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium supplements (مكملات الكالسيوم) are intended for therapeutic use, meaning they are administered to treat or prevent medical conditions related to calcium deficiency or bone health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1592
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon is classified within the functional group “hormones by function” and its disposition is that of a hormone. As a hormone, parathormon produces a specific physiologic effect: it leads to decreased renal Ca++ excretion. This reduction in renal calcium loss is a key component of parathormon’s role in maintaining calcium homeostasis, and recognizing that parathormon belongs to the hormone category and exerts this effect is important for the clinical management of calcium‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate are both classified as calcium compounds, indicating that each belongs to the broader category of calcium‑containing substances. As members of this class, they share the fundamental chemical characteristic of providing bioavailable calcium and interacting with phosphate metabolism. Recognizing calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate as calcium compounds helps clarify their roles in medical nutrition, calcium supplementation, and the management of bone health and phosphate balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a pathology that can be applied to both بيلة سِّيسْتينِية and the radius, indicating that this qualifier is relevant to the disease entity بيلة سِّيسْتينِية as well as to the anatomical structure of the radius bone. This relationship highlights that the same pathological descriptor may be used across different medical contexts, encompassing both a specific disease and a skeletal element.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.
